title: Sass: Engineering Professional Stylesheets

questions.question_text: In the 7-1 pattern, what is the purpose of the 'abstracts/' directory?
options: To store files that don't output CSS themselves (variables, mixins, functions)
To store the final compiled main.css
questions.explanation: Abstracts contain the logic and configuration that other files @use, but they should not generate CSS declarations themselves.

questions.question_text: Why is 'math.div(100%, 3)' preferred over '100% / 3' in modern Sass?
options: Because the '/' operator is being deprecated for mathematical division to avoid ambiguity with CSS slash-syntax
Because it results in a more precise number
There is no preference; they are identical
questions.explanation: Sass is moving towards math.div() as the standard for numeric division.

questions.question_text: What does '@error' do in a Sass mixin?
options: It colors the text red in the browser
It logs a warning to the console but continues build
It immediately stops the compilation and displays an error message
It deletes the current style file
questions.explanation: @error is a fatal directive used to enforce design system constraints.

questions.question_text: Which directive allows you to refer to a parent selector within a nested rule?
questions.explanation: The '&' symbol acts as a reference to the parent selector, enabling BEM modifiers like '&--active'.

questions.question_text: What happens to a Sass Namespace (using @use 'vars') if you don't use 'as *'?
options: Variables must be prefixed with the module name (e.g., vars.$color)
It makes all variables global by default
It creates a new CSS file
questions.explanation: @use provides encapsulation; you must use the prefix unless you explicitly use 'as *'.

questions.question_text: How many levels of nesting are recommended in professional Sass?
questions.explanation: The 'Rule of Three' ensures that CSS selectors remain efficient and readable.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between a Placeholder (%) and a standard Class (.) when not extended?
options: Placeholders do not output any code in the final CSS file if they are not extended
questions.explanation: Placeholders allow you to define 'silent' styles that only exist for logic reuse.

questions.question_text: What is the main advantage of using an '@each' loop?
options: It makes the page load faster
It allows you to iterate over a collection (like a list of colors) to automate class generation
It compiles the code to JavaScript
questions.explanation: Loops automate repetitive tasks, which is the core of programmatic styling.

questions.question_text: What does 'lighten($color, 20%)' return?
options: A version of the color that is 20% closer to white
questions.explanation: lighten() is a built-in Sass color function for manipulating lightness.

questions.question_text: In the BEM naming convention, what does '&__element' generate if the parent is '.card'?
questions.explanation: Double underscores indicate a child element within a block in BEM.

questions.question_text: Why should you use a Mixin instead of @extend for layout logic requiring custom widths?
options: Mixins allow you to pass parameters/arguments, whereas @extend is static
Extends are broken in some browsers
There is no reason; @extend is better
questions.explanation: Mixins are essentially 'functions' that output CSS, providing maximum flexibility.

questions.question_text: What does '@forward' do in a Sass library context?
options: It moves the cursor to the next line
It makes a module's members available as if they were defined in the current file when loaded elsewhere
It deletes previous versions of a file
It skips the current block of code
questions.explanation: @forward is used to aggregate several modules into a single entrypoint.

questions.question_text: Which Sass operator is used for string interpolation (injecting variables into strings or selectors)?
questions.explanation: Interpolation allows you to dynamically build strings or selector names using variables.

questions.question_text: What is the return type of a '@function' in Sass?
options: A single value (number, color, string, etc.)
questions.explanation: Functions are for calculations and must @return a value to be used in a property.

questions.question_text: What is 'Sass Soup'?
options: A derogatory term for messy, over-nested, and unmaintainable Sass code
A new feature in Sass v5
questions.explanation: Over-nesting (4+ levels) leads to 'Sass Soup'—selectors that are too specific and difficult to override.

questions.question_text: Which directive acts as a non-fatal warning to the developer during build?
questions.explanation: @warn provides feedback in the terminal without stopping the build process.

questions.question_text: How do you mix two colors together in Sass?
questions.explanation: The mix() function takes two colors and returns their blend based on weight.

questions.question_text: What is the primary benefit of the 7-1 pattern for Sass?
options: It makes the CSS compiled file smaller
It provides a standard, modular structure that makes large projects easier to manage
It's required by the Sass compiler
questions.explanation: Architectural patterns like 7-1 are about human maintainability and organization.

questions.question_text: Which of these is NOT a control directive in Sass?
questions.explanation: @select is not a Sass directive; the others are part of the 'control flow' suite.

questions.question_text: What is the output of '5px + 10px' in Sass?
questions.explanation: Sass is unit-aware and correctly performs mathematical operations on matching units.
